role_summary: NAACP General Counsel 1956-1968 (successor to Thurgood Marshall); argued for Oliver Brown in Brown v. Board of Education (1954); U.S. District Judge for the Southern District of New York (Nixon appointee, 1972-2012); argued 22 Supreme Court cases and won 21.

photo.alt: Judge Robert L. Carter, official federal judiciary portrait

ai_reading: By the count of how often he is the single nearest neighbor of another interviewee's passages, Carter ranks second in the whole catalog (top neighbor of 12 entries, present in the top five of 33), behind only the Mississippi field organizer Charles McLaurin (entry 17). That places the NAACP's national legal-staff lawyer one step from the most-connected node in a corpus dominated by grassroots organizers, and the embedding treats him as the hub of the litigation cohort: his five strongest neighbors by frequency are the South Carolina civil-rights lawyer Matthew J. Perry Jr. (entry 87, count 311), McLaurin (entry 17, count 215), Harold K. Brown (entry 55), Oliver W. Hill Jr. (entry 94), and the photographer-organizer Cecil J. Williams (entry 14). The unobvious pairing is the second of those. Cosine similarity in this corpus pulls Carter's recorded testimony toward McLaurin's voter-registration fieldwork almost as strongly as toward his fellow courtroom litigators, even though the two men worked at opposite ends of the movement's division of labor. The concept axes converge on the same fact: on the Tactical Pragmatism versus Strategic Vision axis the embedding reads Carter toward the Strategic Vision pole (normalized position -0.38), the placement one would expect for the lawyer who shaped the long-range litigation strategy behind Brown, and one that tracks the way he narrates his own work as the building of a planned, precedent-by-precedent campaign rather than a series of isolated cases.
biographical_paragraph: Born in Caryville, Florida, in 1917 and raised in Newark and East Orange, New Jersey, after his family came north in the Great Migration, Carter took a law degree from Howard University, a second degree from Columbia, and served in the Army Air Forces before joining the legal staff of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People in 1944 [src: 1, 2]. He rose from legal assistant and assistant special counsel to succeed Thurgood Marshall as the organization's General Counsel in 1956, holding the post until 1968; over his NAACP years he argued twenty-two cases before the United States Supreme Court and won twenty-one [src: 2, 3]. His first Supreme Court argument was McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents (1950), in which the Court struck down the separate seating, library table, and dining arrangements imposed on the graduate student George W. McLaurin (no relation to the Mississippi organizer Charles McLaurin, entry 17) [src: 4]. He represented Oliver Brown, lead plaintiff in the consolidated school cases decided as Brown v. Board of Education in 1954, and pressed to ground the argument in Kenneth and Mamie Clark's psychological research on segregation's harm to children [src: 1, 5]. Jack Greenberg (entry 56), who tried the Topeka case alongside him, and Mildred Bond Roxborough (entry 90), who worked on the NAACP staff, both name Carter in their own oral histories. President Richard Nixon nominated him to the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York on June 15, 1972, and the Senate confirmed him on July 21, 1972 [src: 3]. He assumed senior status on December 31, 1986, and remained on the federal bench until his death on January 3, 2012 [src: 3]. His memoir, A Matter of Law: A Memoir of Struggle in the Cause of Equal Rights, appeared in 2005 [src: 6]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 108 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert L. Carter
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling his high-school years, when the school closed its swimming pool rather than let him use it after he insisted on his right to swim with the white students:
interview_snippets.quote: That showed me the great cost to white of segregation. They paid a price for it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert L. Carter
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what debating against other colleges while a student at Lincoln University gave him:
interview_snippets.quote: And I think what that did for me was to enable me to not fear competing with white.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert L. Carter
interview_snippets.lead_in: Carter believed a courtroom lawyer needed a big voice, which he felt he lacked; he describes the method he relied on instead:
interview_snippets.quote: What I had to do was to be able to know the fact and to be able to be conversant with all the facts and the law.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert L. Carter
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the conditions imposed on the graduate student George W. McLaurin, the subject of Carter's first Supreme Court argument, McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents:
interview_snippets.quote: McLaurin was in the same class, in the same faculty. All his segregation was that he was required to sit in a special seat. He was required to sit in a separate table. And he had maybe a rope around him.

interview_snippets.speaker: Jack Greenberg
interview_snippets.lead_in: Jack Greenberg, who joined the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund in 1949, describes the small staff working under Thurgood Marshall:
interview_snippets.quote: He'd won some of the big voting rights case, and also, he had his first assistant who was Robert L. Carter, who was a judge here in New York.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mildred Bond Roxborough
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked why the public pictures the NAACP first as a legal organization, longtime staff member Mildred Bond Roxborough points to Carter's record at the Supreme Court:
interview_snippets.quote: I suspect one of the reasons is, our law has appeared before the Supreme Court so many times. Form of General Counsel Robert Carter, it made some 36 appearances before the Supreme Court, and won about 30 of those cases.
